message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The initial stages of the CoVID-19 coronavirus pandemic all around the world exhibit a nearly exponential rise in the number of infections with time. Planners, governments, and agencies are scrambling to figure out “
                  <jats:italic>How much? How bad?</jats:italic>
                  ” and how to effectively treat the potentially large numbers of simultaneously sick people. Modeling the CoVID-19 pandemic using an exponential rise implicitly assumes a nearly unlimited population of uninfected persons (
                  <jats:italic>“dilute pandemic”</jats:italic>
                  ). Once a significant fraction of the population is infected (
                  <jats:italic>“saturated pandemic”</jats:italic>
                  ), an exponential growth no longer applies. A new model is developed here, which modifies the standard exponential growth function to account for factors such as
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  . A
                  <jats:italic>
                    Social Mitigation Parameter [SMP] α
                    <jats:sub>S</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  is introduced to account for these types of society-wide changes, which can modify the standard exponential growth function, as follows:
                  <jats:disp-formula id="eqn1">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20091207v1_eqn1.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  The
                  <jats:italic>
                    doubling-time t
                    <jats:sub>dbl</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  = (In 2)/
                  <jats:italic>
                    K
                    <jats:sub>o</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  can also be used to substitute for
                  <jats:italic>
                    K
                    <jats:sub>o</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  , giving a {
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>dbl</jats:sub>
                  ,
                  <jats:italic>
                    α
                    <jats:sub>S</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  } parameter pair for comparing to actual CoVID-19 data. This model shows how the number of CoVID-19 infections can self-limit before reaching a
                  <jats:italic>saturated pandemic</jats:italic>
                  level. It also provides estimates for: (a) the timing of the
                  <jats:italic>pandemic peak</jats:italic>
                  , (b) the maximum number of new daily cases that would be expected, and (c) the expected total number of CoVID-19 cases. This model shows fairly good agreement with the presently available CoVID-19 pandemic data for several individual States, and the for the USA as a whole (
                  <jats:italic>6 Figures</jats:italic>
                  ), as well as for various countries around the World (
                  <jats:italic>9 Figures</jats:italic>
                  ). An augmented model with two
                  <jats:italic>
                    Mitigation Parameters, α
                    <jats:sub>S</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>
                    β
                    <jats:sub>S</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  , is also developed, which can give better agreement with the daily new CoVID-19 data. Data-to-model comparisons also indicate that using
                  <jats:italic>
                    α
                    <jats:sub>S</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  by itself likely provides a worst-case estimate, while using both
                  <jats:italic>
                    α
                    <jats:sub>S</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>
                    β
                    <jats:sub>S</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  likely provides a best-case estimate for the CoVID-19 spread.
                </jats:p>
title: Initial Model for the Impact of Social Distancing on CoVID-19 Spread

reference.unstructured: https://www.scmp.com/news/hong-kong/health-environment/article/3075609/coronavirus-can-be-transmitted-through-faeces {South China Morning Post/Hong Kong/Health and Environment}

https://www.MedRxiv.org/ID=MEDRXIV/2020/043752v1, 03.25.2020, “Forecasting COVID-19 impact on hospital bed-days, ICU-days, ventilator-days and deaths by US state in the next 4 months”, IHME COVID-19 Health Service Utilization Forecasting Team.
